Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools are two distinct services. A **User Pool** is a managed user directory that handles user sign-up, sign-in, and account management, and acts as an OpenID Connect (OIDC) identity provider. It issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) upon successful user authentication. In contrast, an **Identity Pool** exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via AWS Security Token Service (STS). Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities, bridging user-facing authentication with direct AWS API access from clients.

Upon a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens:
1.  The **ID token**: A JWT containing user identity claims (e.g., sub, email, name, custom attributes). It identifies the user to the application and is not intended for API authorization.
2.  The **access token**: A JWT containing scopes (e.g., cognito:groups, OAuth scopes) and a `token_use` claim of 'access'. It is meant to be sent with API requests for authorization.
3.  The **refresh token**: An opaque string used to request new access and ID tokens from the `/oauth2/token` endpoint without requiring the user to re-authenticate. Refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years.

results.query: What is the difference between Cognito User Pools and Identity Pools, and what tokens does a User Pool issue on sign-in?

retrieved_chunks.content: Amazon Cognito provides two distinct services that are often confused: Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and federated identity, and issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user authenticates. The User Pool itself is the OIDC identity provider; downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues. An Identity Pool, by contrast, exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, an external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via STS. Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities and are the bridge between an application's user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access from clients. A typical pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, get an ID token, and then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials that let the client (e.g., a mobile app) call S3 or DynamoDB directly. Either service can be used independently: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. The access token is a JWT that contains scopes (cognito:groups, the OAuth scopes granted, and a token_use claim of 'access') and is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization. The refresh token is an opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the /oauth2/token endpoint without re-authenticating; refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked. Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration, configurable per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. Both are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair. The 'aud' claim of the ID token is the app client ID; the 'iss' claim is https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}; the 'token_use' claim is 'id' or 'access'. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

Cognito User Pools implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards, exposing standard endpoints like /oauth2/authorize, /oauth2/token, /oauth2/userInfo, and /.well-known/openid-configuration plus /.well-known/jwks.json. Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

Cognito User Pools support federation with external identity providers in two main flavors. Social federation supports Google, Facebook, Apple, and Amazon out of the box: you register an OAuth app with the provider, add the client ID and secret to the User Pool's identity provider configuration, and choose attribute mappings from the provider's claims to User Pool attributes. Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

To validate a Cognito-issued JWT, an API server fetches the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json. The JWKS contains the public keys (RSA modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, identified by 'kid' (key ID). The validation steps are: parse the JWT header to find the kid, look up the matching key in the JWKS (caching the JWKS in memory and refreshing on a kid miss), verify the RS256 signature, then validate claims: 'iss' must equal the User Pool issuer URL, 'exp' must be in the future, 'token_use' must match the expected token type (e.g., 'access' for API auth), and 'aud' (for ID tokens) or 'client_id' (for access tokens) must match an allowed app client. results.query: How do CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache and how do you invalidate content already in the cache?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront cache behaviors define how the distribution handles requests matching specific path patterns. A distribution always has a default cache behavior (path pattern *), and additional behaviors can be added with patterns like /api/*, /static/*, /*.jpg. Behaviors are matched in priority order, with the default behavior used as a fallback. Each behavior specifies the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy (HTTP, HTTPS, redirect HTTP to HTTPS), cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any function or Lambda@Edge associations. Cache policies define what is included in the cache key (which headers, cookies, query strings affect caching), what TTLs apply (minimum, default, maximum), and whether to enable Gzip / Brotli compression. The TTL can also be controlled by Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin: by default CloudFront respects them, capped by the maximum TTL. Setting Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store on the origin response prevents caching at the edge. Cache invalidation is available via the CreateInvalidation API, which removes paths from the cache; the first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, additional paths cost a small per-path fee. Versioned filenames (e.g., app.v123.js) avoid the need for invalidation by changing the cache key naturally on each release.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

results.answer:
## CloudFront Cache Behaviors: Caching and Invalidation

### What Gets Cached (Cache Keys)

Cache behaviors use **cache policies** to define what is included in the cache key — meaning which elements of a request determine whether a cached copy can be served. The cache key can include:

- **Headers**
- **Cookies**
- **Query strings**

Only the elements explicitly included in the cache policy affect caching; requests that differ only in excluded elements will share the same cached object.

### How Long Content Is Cached (TTLs)

Cache policies also define **TTL (Time-to-Live)** settings:

- **Minimum TTL**
- **Default TTL**
- **Maximum TTL**

By default, CloudFront **respects `Cache-Control` or `Expires` headers** sent by the origin, but caps them at the configured maximum TTL. If the origin sends `Cache-Control: no-cache` or `no-store`, CloudFront will **not cache** the response at the edge.

---

### How Requests Are Matched to Behaviors

Cache behaviors are matched in **priority order** by path pattern (e.g., `/api/*`, `/static/*`, `/*.jpg`). The **default behavior** (path pattern `*`) acts as a fallback if no other pattern matches. Each behavior independently specifies its origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy, and associated cache/origin request policies.

---

### Invalidating Cached Content

Two main approaches exist:

1. **Cache Invalidation API (`CreateInvalidation`):** You can explicitly remove objects from the edge cache by specifying their paths. Pricing note: the **first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free**; additional paths incur a small per-path fee.

2. **Versioned Filenames (Recommended):** By changing the filename on each release (e.g., `app.v123.js`), the cache key naturally changes, making the old cached version irrelevant — **no invalidation needed**. This is the preferred approach to avoid invalidation costs and complexity.

results.answer:
# CloudFront Cache Behaviors and Cache Invalidation

## How Cache Behaviors Decide What to Cache

CloudFront cache behaviors use **cache policies** to determine what gets cached:

- **Cache key composition**: The cache policy defines which elements are included in the cache key—specifically which headers, cookies, and query strings affect caching decisions
- **TTL settings**: Cache policies specify minimum, default, and maximum time-to-live (TTL) values for cached objects
- **Compression**: Policies can enable Gzip and Brotli compression
- **Origin headers**: By default, CloudFront respects `Cache-Control` and `Expires` headers from the origin response, though they are capped by the maximum TTL you've set
- **Prevention of caching**: Setting `Cache-Control: no-cache` or `no-store` on the origin response prevents caching at the edge

## How to Invalidate Cached Content

CloudFront provides the **CreateInvalidation API** to remove paths from the cache:

- **Free tier**: The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free
- **Additional paths**: Beyond 1,000 paths/month, there is a small per-path fee

**Alternative approach**: Instead of relying on invalidation, AWS recommends using **versioned filenames** (e.g., `app.v123.js`). By changing the filename on each release, you naturally change the cache key without needing to manually invalidate anything.

CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache based on the specified cache policies. These policies define what is included in the cache key (which headers, cookies, query strings affect caching), the Time-To-Live (TTL) values (minimum, default, maximum), and whether to enable Gzip or Brotli compression. Additionally, CloudFront respects Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin by default, but these are capped by the maximum TTL. If an origin response has Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store, it prevents caching at the edge.

To invalidate content already in the cache, you can use the CreateInvalidation API, which allows you to remove specific paths from the cache. The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, and any additional paths incur a small fee per path.

results.query: How do CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache and how do you invalidate content already in the cache?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront cache behaviors define how the distribution handles requests matching specific path patterns. A distribution always has a default cache behavior (path pattern *), and additional behaviors can be added with patterns like /api/*, /static/*, /*.jpg. Behaviors are matched in priority order, with the default behavior used as a fallback. Each behavior specifies the origin, allowed HTTP methods, viewer protocol policy (HTTP, HTTPS, redirect HTTP to HTTPS), cache policy, origin request policy, response headers policy, and any function or Lambda@Edge associations. Cache policies define what is included in the cache key (which headers, cookies, query strings affect caching), what TTLs apply (minimum, default, maximum), and whether to enable Gzip / Brotli compression. The TTL can also be controlled by Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin: by default CloudFront respects them, capped by the maximum TTL. Setting Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store on the origin response prevents caching at the edge. Cache invalidation is available via the CreateInvalidation API, which removes paths from the cache; the first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are free, additional paths cost a small per-path fee. Versioned filenames (e.g., app.v123.js) avoid the need for invalidation by changing the cache key naturally on each release.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

results.answer:
CloudFront cache behaviors decide what to cache based on rules defined within them, which are matched against specific URL path patterns. A distribution always has a default cache behavior with the path pattern "*", and additional behaviors can be added with more specific patterns. These behaviors are prioritized, with the default serving as a fallback.

Each cache behavior specifies a cache policy, which determines what is included in the cache key (headers, cookies, query strings that affect caching) and the Time To Live (TTL) for cached objects (minimum, default, and maximum). The TTL can also be influenced by Cache-Control or Expires headers from the origin, though CloudFront's maximum TTL acts as a cap. Responses with Cache-Control: no-cache or no-store from the origin prevent caching at the edge.

To invalidate content already in the cache, you can use the `CreateInvalidation` API. This API removes specified paths from the CloudFront edge cache. The first 1,000 invalidation paths per month are provided for free, with additional paths incurring a small per-path fee. An alternative to manual invalidation is using versioned filenames (e.g., `app.v123.js`). When the filename changes with each release, it naturally alters the cache key, effectively bypassing the need for explicit invalidation.

results.query: Explain the four Lambda@Edge event types and the practical effects of replication on debugging.

retrieved_chunks.content: Lambda@Edge supports four CloudFront event types, each fired at a different point in the request lifecycle. viewer-request runs on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted, so it sees every request and is the right place for redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect cache key. origin-request runs only on cache misses, just before CloudFront forwards to the origin; this is the place to rewrite the URL path or change the origin. origin-response runs after the origin returns a response and before CloudFront caches it; this is where to add or rewrite headers that should be cached, or to handle origin errors with custom responses. viewer-response runs on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits, so it is the right place for headers that must be present on every response (though CloudFront response headers policies are usually a cheaper option). Each event type has different size limits (the viewer-side caps the request body at 40 KB, origin-side at 1 MB) and different latency budgets (viewer-side max 5 seconds, origin-side max 30 seconds). Choosing the right event is critical: putting expensive logic on viewer-request multiplies cost compared to putting it on origin-request.

Lambda@Edge integrates AWS Lambda with CloudFront, letting you run Node.js or Python functions in response to CloudFront events. Functions are authored and uploaded as ordinary Lambda functions in the us-east-1 region, then 'published' to a numbered version and associated with a specific cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront automatically replicates the function to all regional edge caches; replication completes within a few minutes after publishing. Functions can be associated to four event types: viewer-request, origin-request, origin-response, and viewer-response. Lambda@Edge functions execute in the closest AWS region to the viewer (not literally at every edge POP), and they have access to the full Node.js / Python runtime environment and a limited set of npm or pip packages bundled into the deployment. The maximum execution time is 5 seconds for viewer-facing events and 30 seconds for origin events; memory is configurable up to 10 GB. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second of compute, separately from regular Lambda. Common limitations: no environment variables, no VPC access, request body size limits (40 KB for viewer events, 1 MB for origin events), and a smaller set of supported runtimes than regular Lambda.

Lambda@Edge is a feature of Amazon CloudFront that runs AWS Lambda functions at CloudFront points of presence in response to viewer or origin events. Functions are written in Node.js or Python, packaged like ordinary Lambda functions, and must be deployed in the us-east-1 (N. Virginia) region. To use a function with CloudFront, you publish a numbered version (Lambda@Edge does not support $LATEST or aliases) and associate that exact version ARN with a cache behavior on a distribution. CloudFront then replicates the code to all regional edge caches around the world; replication usually completes within a few minutes but can take longer. Each function executes in the AWS region nearest the viewer, not literally at the POP itself. Lambda@Edge can modify the request and response that flows between the viewer, CloudFront, and the origin; it cannot serve traffic without CloudFront. Use cases include header rewrites, A/B testing logic, JWT validation, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device, image resizing on demand, custom auth, and request normalization. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second separately from regular Lambda, and pricing is higher than standard Lambda because of the global replication infrastructure.

CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are two different ways to run code at the CloudFront layer, with very different tradeoffs. CloudFront Functions are lightweight, JavaScript-only (ECMAScript 5.1 plus a subset of newer features) functions that execute in microseconds at the edge POP itself (not a regional edge cache), with a maximum execution time of about 1 ms and 2 MB of memory. They support only viewer-request and viewer-response event types, have no network or filesystem access, and cannot make outbound calls. They cost roughly 1/6 the price of Lambda@Edge per request and are designed for high-volume, low-latency request manipulation: header rewrites, simple URL normalization, redirects, A/B test cookie assignment, JWT validation against a static key, basic authorization checks. Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory and 5–30 second timeouts, supports outbound network calls (including to other AWS services or third-party APIs), and works on origin-request and origin-response events as well as viewer events. Use CloudFront Functions for hot-path, stateless transformations and Lambda@Edge for anything requiring external lookups, larger code, or origin-side logic.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda: the first invocation in a region after a long idle period requires the runtime and code to be loaded, which can add hundreds of milliseconds for Node.js and somewhat more for Python. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

results.answer:
## Lambda@Edge Event Types

Lambda@Edge supports four event types, each firing at a distinct point in the request/response lifecycle:

### 1. `viewer-request`
- Fires on **every request from a viewer**, before the CloudFront cache is consulted
- Sees 100% of all incoming requests (including cache hits)
- Best for: redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should affect the cache key
- **Limits:** 40 KB request body, 5-second max execution time
- ⚠️ Expensive placement — putting heavy logic here multiplies cost significantly compared to `origin-request`

### 2. `origin-request`
- Fires **only on cache misses**, just before CloudFront forwards the request to the origin
- Best for: rewriting URL paths, changing the origin dynamically, A/B testing logic, dynamic origin selection by geography or device
- **Limits:** 1 MB request body, 30-second max execution time

### 3. `origin-response`
- Fires **after the origin returns a response**, before CloudFront caches it
- Best for: adding or rewriting headers that should be cached, handling origin errors with custom responses
- **Limits:** 1 MB body, 30-second max execution time

### 4. `viewer-response`
- Fires on **every response sent to the viewer**, including cache hits
- Best for: headers that must be present on every response
- Note: CloudFront response headers policies are often a cheaper alternative for simple header injection
- **Limits:** 40 KB body, 5-second max execution time

---

## Practical Effects of Replication on Debugging

Replication creates several notable debugging challenges:

1. **Geographically scattered logs:** Because Lambda@Edge executes in whichever AWS region is *nearest the viewer*, log groups are created in CloudWatch across **many different regions**. A single function can have logs scattered across dozens of regions, making it difficult to locate a specific invocation or trace an issue.

2. **No centralized log search:** While CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for a high-level view, log searches generally require **querying each region individually**, which is time-consuming and operationally complex.

3. **Cold starts with no mitigation:** Cold start behavior is similar to regular Lambda — the first invocation in a region after a long idle period can add **hundreds of milliseconds** of latency (more for Python than Node.js). Crucially, **Provisioned Concurrency is not available** for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to maintain steady traffic across all regions.

4. **Immutable replicas complicate cleanup:** Replicated function versions are read-only and cannot be deleted directly. To remove a function, you must first dissociate it from all distributions/behaviors, wait for replicas to be cleaned up (typically a few hours or longer), and only then delete the underlying function version — making rollback and cleanup more cumbersome.

results.answer:
# Lambda@Edge Event Types

The four event types fire at different points in the request lifecycle:

1. **viewer-request**: Fires on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted. Ideal for redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that affects cache key.

2. **origin-request**: Fires only on cache misses, just before CloudFront forwards to the origin. The right place to rewrite URL paths or change the origin.

3. **origin-response**: Fires after the origin returns a response and before CloudFront caches it. Used to add or rewrite headers that should be cached, or to handle origin errors with custom responses.

4. **viewer-response**: Fires on every response sent to the viewer, including cache hits. The right place for headers that must be present on every response (though CloudFront response headers policies are usually cheaper).

## Event-Specific Constraints

These event types have different operational constraints:
- **Viewer-side events** (viewer-request, viewer-response): 40 KB request body limit, 5 second max execution time
- **Origin-side events** (origin-request, origin-response): 1 MB request body limit, 30 second max execution time

The choice of event type is critical to cost efficiency—putting expensive logic on viewer-request multiplies costs compared to origin-request.

# Practical Debugging Effects of Replication

Lambda@Edge replication creates significant debugging challenges:

- **Distributed logs**: CloudWatch log groups exist in many regions. A single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation.
- **Regional queries required**: Log searches generally require querying each region individually rather than a single centralized location.
- **CloudWatch metrics workaround**: Global metric aggregation is available for at-a-glance views, but this doesn't solve the log fragmentation problem.

These replication dynamics make troubleshooting Lambda@Edge functions more complex than standard Lambda.

Lambda@Edge can modify the request and response that flows between the viewer, CloudFront, and the origin; it cannot serve traffic without CloudFront. Use cases include header rewrites, A/B testing logic, JWT validation, dynamic origin selection based on geography or device, image resizing on demand, custom auth, and request normalization. Lambda@Edge is billed per request and per GB-second separately from regular Lambda, and pricing is higher than standard Lambda because of the global replication infrastructure.

CloudFront Functions and Lambda@Edge are two different ways to run code at the CloudFront layer, with very different tradeoffs. CloudFront Functions are lightweight, JavaScript-only (ECMAScript 5.1 plus a subset of newer features) functions that execute in microseconds at the edge POP itself (not a regional edge cache), with a maximum execution time of about 1 ms and 2 MB of memory. They support only viewer-request and viewer-response event types, have no network or filesystem access, and cannot make outbound calls. They cost roughly 1/6 the price of Lambda@Edge per request and are designed for high-volume, low-latency request manipulation: header rewrites, simple URL normalization, redirects, A/B test cookie assignment, JWT validation against a static key, basic authorization checks. Lambda@Edge runs full Node.js or Python with up to 10 GB memory and 5–30 second timeouts, supports outbound network calls (including to other AWS services or third-party APIs), and works on origin-request and origin-response events as well as viewer events. Use CloudFront Functions for hot-path, stateless transformations and Lambda@Edge for anything requiring external lookups, larger code, or origin-side logic.

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, CloudFront replicates the function from us-east-1 to all regional edge caches around the world. The replicated copies are read-only; you cannot delete a replicated version directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, first dissociate it from every distribution and behavior, then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up (this typically takes a few hours but can take longer), and only then can the underlying function version be deleted. Provisioned concurrency is not available for Lambda@Edge, so the only way to keep functions warm is to receive steady traffic across regions. Because the function runs in whichever region is nearest the viewer, log groups exist in many CloudWatch regions, and a single function may have logs scattered across dozens of regions, complicating debugging and aggregation. CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for an at-a-glance view, but log searches generally require querying each region.

results.answer:
The four Lambda@Edge event types are:

1. **viewer-request**: This event triggers on every request from a viewer before CloudFront consults the cache. It is the appropriate place for operations like redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that influences the cache key.

2. **origin-request**: This event occurs only on cache misses, right before CloudFront forwards the request to the origin. It's used to rewrite the URL path or to modify the origin.

3. **origin-response**: This event is invoked after the origin has returned a response and before CloudFront caches the response. It's suitable for adding or rewriting headers that need to be cached, or for handling origin errors with custom responses.

4. **viewer-response**: This event runs on every response sent back to the viewer, including cache hits. It's the right place for adding headers that must be included with every response, although using CloudFront response headers policies is usually more cost-effective.

Regarding the practical effects of replication on debugging:

When a Lambda@Edge function is associated with a CloudFront cache behavior, it is replicated from the us-east-1 region to all regional edge caches worldwide. These replicated copies are read-only, meaning you cannot delete or modify them directly. To remove a function from CloudFront, you must first dissociate it from all distributions and behaviors, and then wait for the replicas to be cleaned up, which can take several hours or longer.

This replication can complicate debugging. Since the function executes in the AWS region closest to the viewer, log groups for the function can exist in many CloudWatch regions. As a result, logs may be scattered across numerous regions, making it challenging to aggregate and analyze them comprehensively. While CloudWatch metrics can be aggregated globally for a general overview, performing log searches typically requires querying each individual region, which can add complexity and time to the debugging process.

Lambda@Edge supports four CloudFront event types:

1.  **viewer-request:** This event fires on every request from a viewer before the cache is consulted. It's suitable for actions like redirects, viewer-side authentication, and request normalization that should influence the cache key.

This means that log groups exist in many different CloudWatch regions, and logs for a single function can be scattered across numerous regions. results.query: How does an API validate a Cognito-issued JWT, and what claims must it check?

retrieved_chunks.content: To validate a Cognito-issued JWT, an API server fetches the JSON Web Key Set (JWKS) from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json. The JWKS contains the public keys (RSA modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, identified by 'kid' (key ID). The validation steps are: parse the JWT header to find the kid, look up the matching key in the JWKS (caching the JWKS in memory and refreshing on a kid miss), verify the RS256 signature, then validate claims: 'iss' must equal the User Pool issuer URL, 'exp' must be in the future, 'token_use' must match the expected token type (e.g., 'access' for API auth), and 'aud' (for ID tokens) or 'client_id' (for access tokens) must match an allowed app client. Cognito does not currently support full token revocation for the access and ID tokens (they are valid until expiry); only the refresh token can be revoked, which prevents new access tokens from being issued. Many libraries (aws-jwt-verify, jose, python-jose, PyJWT) implement these checks; rolling your own validator is an easy place to introduce auth bugs.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. The access token is a JWT that contains scopes (cognito:groups, the OAuth scopes granted, and a token_use claim of 'access') and is meant to be sent on API requests for authorization. The refresh token is an opaque string used to request fresh access and ID tokens from the /oauth2/token endpoint without re-authenticating; refresh tokens have a configurable lifetime from 1 hour to 10 years and can be revoked. Both ID and access tokens have a default 1-hour expiration, configurable per app client between 5 minutes and 24 hours. Both are signed with RS256 using a User Pool-specific key pair. The 'aud' claim of the ID token is the app client ID; the 'iss' claim is https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}; the 'token_use' claim is 'id' or 'access'. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

Cognito User Pools implement OAuth 2.0 and OpenID Connect (OIDC) standards, exposing standard endpoints like /oauth2/authorize, /oauth2/token, /oauth2/userInfo, and /.well-known/openid-configuration plus /.well-known/jwks.json. Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

Amazon Cognito provides two distinct services that are often confused: Cognito User Pools and Cognito Identity Pools. A User Pool is a managed user directory that handles sign-up, sign-in, password reset, MFA, and federated identity, and issues OAuth 2.0 / OIDC tokens (ID, access, and refresh tokens) once a user authenticates. The User Pool itself is the OIDC identity provider; downstream applications validate the JWTs it issues. An Identity Pool, by contrast, exchanges authenticated identity tokens (from a User Pool, an external IdP like Google or Facebook, or a SAML provider) for short-lived AWS credentials via STS. Identity Pools support both authenticated and unauthenticated (guest) identities and are the bridge between an application's user-facing authentication and direct AWS API access from clients. A typical pattern is to authenticate users against a User Pool, get an ID token, and then exchange that token at an Identity Pool for temporary IAM credentials that let the client (e.g., a mobile app) call S3 or DynamoDB directly. Either service can be used independently: User Pools alone for token-based API auth, Identity Pools alone for federated AWS access without a User Pool.

Cognito User Pools support federation with external identity providers in two main flavors. Social federation supports Google, Facebook, Apple, and Amazon out of the box: you register an OAuth app with the provider, add the client ID and secret to the User Pool's identity provider configuration, and choose attribute mappings from the provider's claims to User Pool attributes. Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Federated users are created lazily on first sign-in and have a 'sub' that is unique within the User Pool; the original IdP identity is recorded in the user's identities attribute. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

results.answer:
## Validating a Cognito-Issued JWT

Based on the retrieved documentation, here is the complete process an API server must follow:

---

### Step 1: Fetch the Public Keys (JWKS)

The API server fetches the **JSON Web Key Set (JWKS)** from the User Pool's discovery endpoint:

```
https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json
```

- The JWKS contains the **RSA public keys** (modulus and exponent) used to sign tokens, each identified by a **`kid` (key ID)**.
- The JWKS should be **cached in memory** and only refreshed upon a `kid` miss (i.e., when the token's `kid` isn't found in the cached keys).

---

### Step 2: Match the Key and Verify the Signature

1. **Parse the JWT header** to extract the `kid`.
2. **Look up the matching key** in the JWKS using that `kid`.
3. **Verify the RS256 signature** using the matched public key.

---

### Step 3: Validate the Required Claims

After signature verification, the following claims **must** be checked:

| Claim | Requirement |
|-------|-------------|
| **`iss`** | Must equal the User Pool issuer URL: `https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}` |
| **`exp`** | Must be in the future (token not expired) |
| **`token_use`** | Must match the expected token type — e.g., `"access"` for API authorization, or `"id"` for identity |
| **`aud`** | For **ID tokens**: must match an allowed app client ID |
| **`client_id`** | For **access tokens**: must match an allowed app client |

---

### Important Caveats

- **Use the access token for API authorization**, not the ID token. The ID token is intended to identify the user to the application, while the access token (with its scopes) is meant for authorizing API calls.
- **Token revocation is limited**: Cognito does not support full revocation of access and ID tokens — they remain valid until expiry. Only the **refresh token** can be revoked.
- **Use a well-tested library** (e.g., `aws-jwt-verify`, `jose`, `python-jose`, `PyJWT`) rather than rolling your own validator, as custom implementations are an easy place to introduce auth bugs.

Once configured, the Hosted UI displays a sign-in-with-{provider} button, and on first login Cognito creates a federated user in the User Pool linked to the external identity. SAML federation supports any SAML 2.0 IdP (Okta, Azure AD, ADFS, Auth0, etc.): you upload the IdP metadata XML or URL, configure attribute mappings, and Cognito acts as the SAML SP. OIDC federation supports any OIDC-compliant IdP: you supply the issuer URL, client ID, secret, and authorized scopes. Federated users are created lazily on first sign-in and have a 'sub' that is unique within the User Pool; the original IdP identity is recorded in the user's identities attribute. Multiple IdPs can be enabled simultaneously; the Hosted UI shows one button per enabled provider plus the standard username/password form if local sign-in is allowed.

results.answer:
# Validating Cognito-issued JWTs

Based on the retrieved documentation, here's how an API should validate a Cognito-issued JWT:

## Validation Process

1. **Fetch the JWKS**: Retrieve the JSON Web Key Set from the User Pool's discovery endpoint at:
   ```
   https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}/.well-known/jwks.json
   ```

2. **Parse the JWT header**: Extract the `kid` (key ID) from the token header.

3. **Look up the public key**: Find the matching key in the JWKS using the `kid`. Cache the JWKS in memory and refresh it if a `kid` is not found.

4. **Verify the signature**: Validate the RS256 signature using the public key (RSA modulus and exponent).

## Required Claims to Validate

After signature verification, the API must validate these claims:

- **`iss`** (issuer): Must equal the User Pool issuer URL (`https://cognito-idp.{region}.amazonaws.com/{userPoolId}`)
- **`exp`** (expiration): Must be in the future
- **`token_use`**: Must match the expected token type (e.g., `'access'` for API authorization, `'id'` for identity)
- **`aud`** (for ID tokens): Must match an allowed app client ID
- **`client_id`** (for access tokens): Must match an allowed app client ID

## Best Practice

The documentation strongly recommends using established libraries like `aws-jwt-verify`, `jose`, `python-jose`, or `PyJWT` rather than implementing your own validator, as custom implementations are prone to authentication bugs.

After a successful sign-in, a Cognito User Pool issues three tokens. The ID token is a JWT that contains user identity claims (sub, email, name, custom attributes, cognito:groups) and is intended to identify the user to the application; it is not meant to authorize API calls. APIs validating tokens should always check signature, expiration, issuer, audience, and token_use.

Applications register one or more app clients on the User Pool, each with its own client ID and optional client secret, allowed OAuth flows (authorization code, implicit, client credentials), allowed scopes (openid, profile, email, custom scopes), and allowed callback / logout URLs. The authorization code flow with PKCE is the recommended flow for browser-based and mobile clients; the implicit flow is deprecated for interactive use. Client credentials flow is used for machine-to-machine integrations where there is no user, and requires a client secret. The token endpoint can return ID tokens (claims about the user, signed JWT), access tokens (signed JWT used for API authorization), and refresh tokens (opaque, used to obtain new access and ID tokens without re-prompting the user). All tokens are signed with RS256, and the public key is published at the JWKS endpoint so APIs can validate signatures offline.

results.query: Explain ECS Service Discovery via Cloud Map and how it differs from putting services behind an ALB.

retrieved_chunks.content: ECS Service Discovery integrates with AWS Cloud Map to publish DNS records for tasks in a service so other services can discover them by hostname. When you enable service discovery on an ECS service, ECS registers each task in a Cloud Map service that is part of a private namespace (Route 53 private hosted zone) or a public namespace. Two record types are supported: A records (for awsvpc mode tasks, mapping the service hostname to each task's ENI private IP) and SRV records (which include port info, useful for bridge or host mode tasks where multiple tasks may share a host with different ports). When tasks are added or removed, ECS updates Cloud Map automatically. Other services can resolve service.namespace and get DNS-load-balanced answers across the registered tasks. Service Discovery is a lightweight alternative to a service mesh and a load balancer for east-west traffic where the client is happy to do DNS-level load balancing. For more sophisticated traffic management (retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability), AWS App Mesh or service mesh tools layer on top. Pricing covers Cloud Map API calls and Route 53 DNS queries; both are inexpensive for typical service counts.

ECS supports two ways to launch tasks: standalone tasks via RunTask, and long-running services. RunTask launches a single instance of a task definition that runs to completion (or until stopped) and is appropriate for batch jobs, scheduled tasks via EventBridge, or one-off operations. A service is a controller that maintains a desired count of running tasks, replaces failed tasks, performs rolling deployments when the task definition is updated, and registers/deregisters tasks with one or more target groups behind an ALB or NLB. Service deployments support rolling updates (the default, controlled by minimumHealthyPercent and maximumPercent), blue/green via CodeDeploy (a separate green task set is created and traffic is shifted on success), and external deployment controllers. Services can use ECS Service Discovery (Cloud Map) to register tasks under DNS names, capacity providers to spread tasks across multiple compute strategies (e.g., Fargate + Fargate Spot), and auto-scaling policies based on CloudWatch metrics or target tracking. Services have circuit breakers that automatically roll back a deployment if too many tasks fail health checks consecutively. Choose RunTask for ephemeral work and services for anything that must keep running.

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's container orchestration service. The top-level resource is a cluster, a logical grouping in which tasks and services run. A cluster does not by itself reserve any compute; it can host tasks that run on EC2 instances registered to the cluster (EC2 launch type) or on the AWS-managed Fargate compute engine (Fargate launch type, no instance management required). Tasks are the unit of execution: a task is one or more co-scheduled containers defined by a task definition. ECS schedules tasks onto available capacity according to placement strategies (binpack, random, spread by attribute) and constraints (instance type, AMI, custom attributes). ECS integrates with IAM for task and execution roles, with VPC for networking, with CloudWatch for logs and metrics, with ECR for image storage, and with Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer for ingress. ECS clusters are free; you pay only for the compute (EC2 instance hours or Fargate vCPU/memory seconds) plus any associated data transfer and load balancer charges. ECS competes with EKS (Kubernetes) and is generally simpler to operate, with the tradeoff of being AWS-specific.

ECS supports two launch types that differ fundamentally in compute responsibility. The EC2 launch type runs tasks on EC2 instances that you manage as part of the cluster: you choose instance types, capacity, autoscaling rules, AMI (typically the ECS-optimized Amazon Linux 2023 AMI), patch cadence, and SSH access. The ECS agent on each instance registers it with the cluster and accepts task placements. EC2 launch type is more cost-effective at scale (especially with Reserved Instances or Spot), supports daemons that must run on every host, and allows custom AMIs for specific kernel modules or tooling, but requires capacity planning and OS patching. The Fargate launch type abstracts away EC2 entirely: you specify CPU and memory at the task level, ECS provisions the right amount of compute, and you pay per vCPU-second and GB-second the task runs. Fargate has no host to patch, automatically isolates each task at the kernel level (each task gets its own dedicated VM), and scales to zero when no tasks are running. Tradeoffs: Fargate has limited customization, generally costs more per vCPU-hour than equivalent EC2, has longer task start times (commonly 30–60 seconds versus a few seconds on a warmed EC2 instance), and supports a subset of network and storage features.

An Application Load Balancer (ALB) is a layer 7 load balancer in the AWS Elastic Load Balancing family, designed for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. It terminates TLS, parses requests at the application layer, and routes them to backend targets based on rules that consider the request path, host header, HTTP method, query parameters, source IP, and HTTP headers. An ALB consists of a load balancer (the deployed entity with one or more public or internal IPs in each Availability Zone), one or more listeners (each on a TCP port like 80 or 443), and one or more target groups (the actual backends). The ALB integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free TLS certificates, supports HTTP/2 and gRPC, supports WebSocket upgrades, and can authenticate users via Cognito or any OIDC IdP before forwarding the request. ALBs scale automatically with traffic and are billed by the hour plus by Load Balancer Capacity Units (LCUs), a composite metric covering new connections, active connections, processed bytes, and rule evaluations. ALBs are the right choice for HTTP/HTTPS workloads needing path-based or host-based routing; for raw TCP/UDP/TLS-passthrough at scale, Network Load Balancer (NLB) is the alternative.

results.answer:
## ECS Service Discovery via Cloud Map

### How It Works

ECS Service Discovery integrates with **AWS Cloud Map** to automatically publish DNS records for tasks as they are added or removed from a service. Here's the mechanism:

- ECS registers each task in a Cloud Map service that belongs to either a **private namespace** (backed by a Route 53 private hosted zone) or a **public namespace**.
- Two DNS record types are supported:
  - **A records** – used with `awsvpc` network mode; maps the service hostname directly to each task's ENI private IP.
  - **SRV records** – includes port information; useful for `bridge` or `host` network mode where multiple tasks may share a host but use different ports.
- As tasks are launched or stopped, ECS **automatically updates Cloud Map**, keeping DNS records current.
- Other services resolve the hostname (e.g., `service.namespace`) and receive DNS answers that effectively load balance across all registered task IPs.

---

### How It Differs from an ALB

| Dimension | Service Discovery (Cloud Map) | Application Load Balancer (ALB) |
|---|---|---|
| **Layer** | DNS-level (Layer 3/4) | HTTP/HTTPS (Layer 7) |
| **Load balancing** | Client-side, DNS-based | Centralized, server-side |
| **Traffic routing** | Simple round-robin via DNS | Path-based, host-based, header/method-based rules |
| **TLS termination** | No | Yes (integrates with ACM) |
| **Health checks & failover** | Basic (ECS deregisters stopped tasks) | Advanced (HTTP health checks, circuit breakers) |
| **Advanced features** | None | Auth via Cognito/OIDC, HTTP/2, gRPC, WebSocket |
| **Use case** | East-west (service-to-service) traffic | North-south (external ingress) or complex routing |
| **Cost** | Cloud Map API calls + Route 53 DNS queries (inexpensive) | Hourly charge + LCU-based pricing |
| **Traffic management** | No retries, circuit breaking, or mTLS | Limited; needs App Mesh for those features |

---

### When to Choose Which

- **Service Discovery** is a lightweight option for **east-west (internal) traffic** between microservices where DNS-level load balancing is sufficient and you want to avoid the overhead and cost of a load balancer.
- **ALB** is the right choice when you need **sophisticated HTTP routing**, TLS termination, user authentication, or when handling **external (north-south) traffic** from the internet.
- For even more advanced needs on top of Service Discovery (retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability), **AWS App Mesh** or another service mesh can be layered on top — something an ALB alone does not provide.

retrieved_chunks.content: ECS Service Discovery integrates with AWS Cloud Map to publish DNS records for tasks in a service so other services can discover them by hostname. When you enable service discovery on an ECS service, ECS registers each task in a Cloud Map service that is part of a private namespace (Route 53 private hosted zone) or a public namespace. Two record types are supported: A records (for awsvpc mode tasks, mapping the service hostname to each task's ENI private IP) and SRV records (which include port info, useful for bridge or host mode tasks where multiple tasks may share a host with different ports). When tasks are added or removed, ECS updates Cloud Map automatically. Other services can resolve service.namespace and get DNS-load-balanced answers across the registered tasks. Service Discovery is a lightweight alternative to a service mesh and a load balancer for east-west traffic where the client is happy to do DNS-level load balancing. For more sophisticated traffic management (retries, circuit breaking, mTLS, observability), AWS App Mesh or service mesh tools layer on top. Pricing covers Cloud Map API calls and Route 53 DNS queries; both are inexpensive for typical service counts.

ECS supports two ways to launch tasks: standalone tasks via RunTask, and long-running services. RunTask launches a single instance of a task definition that runs to completion (or until stopped) and is appropriate for batch jobs, scheduled tasks via EventBridge, or one-off operations. A service is a controller that maintains a desired count of running tasks, replaces failed tasks, performs rolling deployments when the task definition is updated, and registers/deregisters tasks with one or more target groups behind an ALB or NLB. Service deployments support rolling updates (the default, controlled by minimumHealthyPercent and maximumPercent), blue/green via CodeDeploy (a separate green task set is created and traffic is shifted on success), and external deployment controllers. Services can use ECS Service Discovery (Cloud Map) to register tasks under DNS names, capacity providers to spread tasks across multiple compute strategies (e.g., Fargate + Fargate Spot), and auto-scaling policies based on CloudWatch metrics or target tracking. Services have circuit breakers that automatically roll back a deployment if too many tasks fail health checks consecutively. Choose RunTask for ephemeral work and services for anything that must keep running.

Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service) is AWS's container orchestration service. The top-level resource is a cluster, a logical grouping in which tasks and services run. A cluster does not by itself reserve any compute; it can host tasks that run on EC2 instances registered to the cluster (EC2 launch type) or on the AWS-managed Fargate compute engine (Fargate launch type, no instance management required). Tasks are the unit of execution: a task is one or more co-scheduled containers defined by a task definition. ECS schedules tasks onto available capacity according to placement strategies (binpack, random, spread by attribute) and constraints (instance type, AMI, custom attributes). ECS integrates with IAM for task and execution roles, with VPC for networking, with CloudWatch for logs and metrics, with ECR for image storage, and with Application Load Balancer or Network Load Balancer for ingress. ECS clusters are free; you pay only for the compute (EC2 instance hours or Fargate vCPU/memory seconds) plus any associated data transfer and load balancer charges. ECS competes with EKS (Kubernetes) and is generally simpler to operate, with the tradeoff of being AWS-specific.

ECS supports two launch types that differ fundamentally in compute responsibility. The EC2 launch type runs tasks on EC2 instances that you manage as part of the cluster: you choose instance types, capacity, autoscaling rules, AMI (typically the ECS-optimized Amazon Linux 2023 AMI), patch cadence, and SSH access. The ECS agent on each instance registers it with the cluster and accepts task placements. EC2 launch type is more cost-effective at scale (especially with Reserved Instances or Spot), supports daemons that must run on every host, and allows custom AMIs for specific kernel modules or tooling, but requires capacity planning and OS patching. The Fargate launch type abstracts away EC2 entirely: you specify CPU and memory at the task level, ECS provisions the right amount of compute, and you pay per vCPU-second and GB-second the task runs. Fargate has no host to patch, automatically isolates each task at the kernel level (each task gets its own dedicated VM), and scales to zero when no tasks are running. Tradeoffs: Fargate has limited customization, generally costs more per vCPU-hour than equivalent EC2, has longer task start times (commonly 30–60 seconds versus a few seconds on a warmed EC2 instance), and supports a subset of network and storage features.

An Application Load Balancer (ALB) is a layer 7 load balancer in the AWS Elastic Load Balancing family, designed for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. It terminates TLS, parses requests at the application layer, and routes them to backend targets based on rules that consider the request path, host header, HTTP method, query parameters, source IP, and HTTP headers. An ALB consists of a load balancer (the deployed entity with one or more public or internal IPs in each Availability Zone), one or more listeners (each on a TCP port like 80 or 443), and one or more target groups (the actual backends). The ALB integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free TLS certificates, supports HTTP/2 and gRPC, supports WebSocket upgrades, and can authenticate users via Cognito or any OIDC IdP before forwarding the request. ALBs scale automatically with traffic and are billed by the hour plus by Load Balancer Capacity Units (LCUs), a composite metric covering new connections, active connections, processed bytes, and rule evaluations. ALBs are the right choice for HTTP/HTTPS workloads needing path-based or host-based routing; for raw TCP/UDP/TLS-passthrough at scale, Network Load Balancer (NLB) is the alternative.

results.answer:
# ECS Service Discovery via Cloud Map vs. ALB

## ECS Service Discovery (Cloud Map)

**How it works:**
- ECS integrates with AWS Cloud Map to automatically publish DNS records for tasks in a service
- When you enable service discovery on an ECS service, ECS registers each task in a Cloud Map service within a private namespace (Route 53 private hosted zone) or public namespace
- Supports two record types:
  - **A records**: Map the service hostname to each task's ENI private IP (for awsvpc mode)
  - **SRV records**: Include port information (useful for bridge or host mode where multiple tasks share a host with different ports)
- DNS updates happen automatically as tasks are added or removed
- Other services resolve `service.namespace` and receive DNS-load-balanced answers across registered tasks

## Key Differences

| Aspect | Service Discovery (Cloud Map) | ALB |
|--------|------|-----|
| **Layer** | DNS-level load balancing | Layer 7 (Application layer) |
| **Use Case** | East-west traffic (service-to-service) | North-south traffic (external ingress) |
| **Complexity** | Lightweight alternative to service mesh/load balancer | Full-featured HTTP/HTTPS routing |
| **Traffic Management** | Client performs DNS-level load balancing | Advanced: path-based routing, host-based routing, HTTP header inspection, TLS termination |
| **Advanced Features** | Basic DNS resolution | Supports retries, authentication (Cognito/OIDC), HTTP/2, gRPC, WebSocket upgrades |
| **When to Choose** | When clients are happy with DNS load balancing | Need sophisticated routing, TLS termination, request inspection, or external access |

**Summary:** Service Discovery is lightweight for internal service-to-service communication, while ALB provides robust application-layer load balancing for external traffic and complex routing needs.

results.answer:
ECS Service Discovery integrates with AWS Cloud Map to publish DNS records for tasks in an ECS service, allowing other services to discover them by hostname. When service discovery is enabled, ECS registers each task within a Cloud Map service associated with either a private or public namespace. It supports two record types: A records for awsvpc mode tasks, which link the service hostname to each task's Elastic Network Interface (ENI) private IP, and SRV records that include port information, which is useful for bridge or host mode tasks where multiple tasks might share a host with different ports.

Service Discovery provides lightweight DNS-level load balancing, allowing other services to resolve service.namespace and receive load-balanced answers across registered tasks. It is seen as an alternative to both service meshes and load balancers for east-west traffic (internal service-to-service communication) when the client can manage load balancing through DNS.

In contrast, putting services behind an Application Load Balancer (ALB) is suited for HTTP and HTTPS traffic. ALBs provide advanced routing features, including the ability to terminate TLS, parse requests at the application layer, and route traffic based on various rules, including request path and headers. ALBs also handle scaling automatically based on traffic and can integrate with AWS services for added features like TLS certificate management and user authentication.

The key differences are:
1. **Load Balancing Mechanism**: ECS Service Discovery relies on DNS-level load balancing, while ALBs provide layer 7 application-level load balancing with more sophisticated routing features.
2. **Use Case**: Service Discovery is a lightweight solution for internal communication (east-west traffic), whereas ALBs are designed for external HTTP/HTTPS traffic with advanced management and scaling capabilities.
3. **Overhead**: Service Discovery is more cost-effective for light usage, while ALBs incur additional charges based on Load Balancer Capacity Units (LCUs) and are generally used for more complex traffic management scenarios.

results.query: How would you serve a private S3 bucket through CloudFront where viewers must sign in with Cognito before fetching content?

retrieved_chunks.content: CloudFront supports two ways to grant time-limited access to private content: signed URLs and signed cookies. Both rely on a CloudFront key group containing one or more public keys; the corresponding private key is held by a trusted signer who creates the signature. A signed URL embeds the policy and signature in the URL itself as query parameters and is therefore tied to a single object (or a narrow URL pattern with a custom policy). Signed cookies set CloudFront-Policy, CloudFront-Signature, and CloudFront-Key-Pair-Id cookies on the viewer's browser; subsequent requests within the same domain automatically include the cookies, allowing access to many objects without changing the URL of each one. Use signed URLs when the client cannot accept cookies (e.g., a media player or a download link), or when access is for a single object. Use signed cookies when distributing access to many files such as paginated content, an HLS playlist with many segments, or an authenticated subdirectory of a website. Both support a 'canned policy' (one URL, one expiration) and a 'custom policy' (multiple URLs or path patterns, IP range restrictions, start time as well as expiration). Trusted signers must be configured per cache behavior, and the cache behavior must have 'Restrict viewer access' enabled.

CloudFront supports several origin types: an S3 bucket (using either the REST endpoint or the website endpoint), a custom HTTP/HTTPS origin (which can be an ALB, NLB, EC2 instance, API Gateway, MediaPackage, or any internet-reachable HTTP server), and a CloudFront origin group for failover between two origins. To restrict a private S3 bucket so it is only reachable through a specific CloudFront distribution, use Origin Access Control (OAC). OAC is the modern replacement for Origin Access Identity (OAI) and supports SigV4 signing, all S3 regions including those launched after 2022, KMS-encrypted objects, and dynamic content methods like PUT and DELETE. With OAC enabled on the origin, CloudFront signs every request to S3 with the distribution's identity, and the bucket policy can be written to allow s3:GetObject only when aws:SourceArn equals the distribution's ARN and Service is cloudfront.amazonaws.com. The bucket itself can stay private with Block Public Access on. For custom origins, CloudFront supports custom headers (sent on every origin request, often used to validate the request originated from CloudFront and not directly from the internet), origin protocol policy (HTTP only, HTTPS only, or match-viewer), and connection timeout / read timeout / keep-alive timeout settings.

Common Lambda@Edge patterns include authentication, A/B testing, header rewrites, and dynamic content selection. For authentication, a viewer-request function can validate a JWT in a cookie or Authorization header against a JWKS, redirect unauthenticated users to a login page (often a Cognito Hosted UI), and forward authenticated requests to the origin with a normalized identity header. For A/B testing, a viewer-request function reads or sets a sticky cookie indicating the experiment bucket, then rewrites the request path or chooses an origin per bucket; the cookie value can also be added to the cache key so each variant is cached separately. For header rewrites, an origin-response function adds security headers (HSTS, CSP), strips sensitive headers, or normalizes Cache-Control to drive CDN behavior. For dynamic content selection, an origin-request function inspects the CloudFront-Viewer-Country header and rewrites the origin or path so users in different countries see different content while sharing a single distribution. Each pattern should be benchmarked because Lambda@Edge cost compounds quickly under high request volume; CloudFront Functions are often a cheaper alternative for simple viewer-side work.

By default, a Cognito User Pool exposes its OAuth endpoints under a Cognito-owned domain like https://{prefix}.auth.{region}.amazoncognito.com, where {prefix} is a unique subdomain claimed when the User Pool's domain is set up. For production applications, a custom domain such as auth.example.com is usually preferred for branding and trust. To use a custom domain, you provide an ACM certificate that must be in us-east-1 regardless of where the User Pool is, and you must own the domain (verified via DNS). The Cognito service then provisions a CloudFront distribution behind the scenes and returns its alias target; you create a DNS A or CNAME record (depending on whether you want to use Route 53 alias) pointing your custom domain to that target. The custom domain serves the same Hosted UI and OAuth endpoints as the default domain, just under your branded host. The parent domain (example.com) must already have an A record before you can create the auth.example.com Cognito custom domain. Custom domain changes can take up to an hour to propagate due to the underlying CloudFront update.

Amazon CloudFront is AWS's content delivery network (CDN) that distributes content from a global network of edge locations to reduce latency for end users. A CloudFront distribution is the top-level resource: it has one or more origins (where the content lives), a set of cache behaviors (rules for how to handle requests matching specific URL patterns), and a globally accessible domain name like d1234abcd.cloudfront.net. Requests from viewers terminate at the nearest edge location, where TLS is negotiated; if the requested object is in the edge cache it is served immediately, otherwise CloudFront fetches it from the origin (often through a regional edge cache that fronts the origin to amplify cache hit rate). CloudFront supports HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2, and HTTP/3 (QUIC). Distributions can serve static assets, dynamic content, video streaming (HLS/DASH), and even APIs. Pricing is based on data transferred out to viewers, number of HTTP/HTTPS requests, and any add-on features like field-level encryption or function executions. CloudFront integrates natively with AWS Certificate Manager for free public TLS certificates, AWS WAF for layer 7 protection, AWS Shield for DDoS protection (Standard included free, Advanced as a paid tier), and Lambda@Edge / CloudFront Functions for request and response manipulation.
